question.text: Under Section 43(5) of the Income-tax Act, exchange-traded currency derivatives transactions carried out on a recognised stock exchange are classified as:

answer.text: Non-speculative business income, and losses can be set off against any normal business income
Speculative business income, and losses can only be set off against speculative income
Income from other sources, taxed at a flat 30%

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The text states: "Section 43(5) has specifically excluded certain derivative transactions from the meaning of speculative transaction... an eligible transaction in respect of trading in derivatives referred to in clause (ac) of section 2 of the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (42 of 1956) carried out in a recognized stock exchange shall not be deemed to be a speculative transaction. Thus, income or loss from dealing in exchange traded currency derivatives shall be deemed as normal business income (non-speculative business)." **The Trap:** The default rule for futures contracts settled without delivery is speculative income. But Section 43(5) carves out exchange-traded derivatives — making them non-speculative. FPIs are an exception: their derivatives income is always capital gains. **Cross-Exam Context:** For FPIs: profits from derivatives = capital gains (always). For all others: exchange-traded currency derivatives = non-speculative PGBP income. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Section 43(5) = non-speculative exception for exchange-traded derivatives. This is critical for tax planning — losses from ETCD can offset general business income, unlike speculative losses.
